subject: English Language
topic: Reading for Maximum Retention and Recall
context: Learning intensive reading strategies using the SQ3R method (Survey, Question, Read, Recite, Review) to preview passages, read carefully, recall salient points, and review for maximum understanding and retention of information on topics like diet, nutrition, and water safety

subject: English Language
topic: Reading for Main and Supporting Ideas
context: Learning to identify the main idea (most important point) and supporting ideas (details that explain or prove the main idea) in passages, understanding how supporting ideas develop and strengthen the main point in paragraphs

subject: English Language
context: Developing comprehension skills by learning to answer different levels of questions: knowledge level (who, what, where, when), translation level (explain, describe), interpretation level (why, how), and projection level (predict, conclude, give opinion)

subject: English Language
context: Learning to identify the author's mood or attitude (anger, happiness, doubt, disappointment, suspicion, excitement) through careful reading of words and expressions, detecting hidden meanings, and understanding the author's presentation style (objective, biased, verbose, terse)

subject: English Language
topic: Reading to Interpret Diagrams, Maps and Sketches
context: Developing skills to read and accurately interpret visual information like diagrams, maps, and sketches that accompany written text, understanding how spatial descriptions complement written material and making connections between text and visuals

subject: English Language
context: Learning to follow written instructions and directions by identifying key directional words (left, right, east, west, northwards, southwards, upwards, towards), reading maps, and following step-by-step instructions accurately

subject: English Language
topic: Writing to Highlight Main and Supporting Ideas
context: Learning to organize writing by identifying and jotting down main and supporting ideas from topics, arranging ideas in logical sequence (beginning, middle, end), and writing appropriate introductions and conclusions for essays and passages

subject: English Language
context: Understanding narrative writing (telling a story with characters, plot, setting) and descriptive writing (using sensory details to describe people, places, things), learning to write compositions with clear structure including introduction, body, and conclusion

subject: English Language
topic: Letter Writing - Formal and Informal Letters
context: Learning the difference between formal letters (two addresses, formal salutation, specific format for official purposes) and informal letters (one address, casual tone for friends and family), understanding appropriate formats and when to use each type

subject: English Language
topic: Speeches - Production of Vowel and Consonant Sounds
context: Learning to listen to and produce clear vowel sounds (a, e, i, o, u) and consonant sounds (t, g, k, l, p, s, etc.) correctly in words, sentences, and passages on topics like family, school, health, and safety

subject: English Language
context: Distinguishing between and correctly pronouncing long vowels (as in sleep, port, leave) and short vowels (as in slip, pot, live), practicing minimal pairs to hear and produce the differences in context

subject: English Language
context: Learning to identify and correctly produce consonant sounds in words and sentences, practicing pronunciation of sounds like /b/ vs /p/ (back/pack), /g/ vs /t/ (goat/toad), /r/ vs /l/ (road/load), /th/ vs /d/ (then/den)

subject: English Language
context: Learning to pronounce consonant clusters (two or more consonants together like in desks, plants, school, string, splash) and diphthongs (two vowel sounds in one syllable like /ei/ in gate, /ou/ in go, /oi/ in oil)

subject: English Language
topic: Word Boundaries, Compound Words and Phrases
context: Understanding linking sounds at word boundaries (matter of, go away), recognizing compound words (blackboard, ice-cream, swimming pool), and learning common phrases (a company of friends, a basket of fruits, a team of players)

subject: English Language
context: Learning to form and respond to question tags correctly (Teachers are punctual, aren't they? Yes, they are), understanding how question tags seek agreement or confirmation, and using appropriate intonation patterns

subject: English Language
context: Developing listening skills to identify main ideas from speeches, stories, and passages on topics like family, school, health, safety, and social issues, learning to extract key information from what is heard

subject: English Language
topic: Parts of Speech - Nouns, Verbs, Adjectives
context: Understanding nouns (naming words for people, places, things), verbs (action or state words), and adjectives (describing words), learning to identify their features and functions in sentences, and making correct sentences using them

subject: English Language
topic: Parts of Speech - Adverbs, Conjunctions, Prepositions
context: Learning about adverbs (words that modify verbs, adjectives, or other adverbs), conjunctions (joining words like and, but, because), and prepositions (words showing relationships like in, on, at, under), and their functions in sentences

subject: English Language
context: Understanding adverbials (words or phrases that tell when, where, how, or why something happens) and tenses (present tense for current actions, past tense for completed actions, future tense for actions yet to happen)

subject: English Language
context: Learning the difference between active voice (subject performs the action: The boy kicked the ball) and passive voice (subject receives the action: The ball was kicked by the boy), and how to change sentences from active to passive

subject: English Language
context: Understanding literature as creative writing including stories, poems, and plays that reflect culture and values, learning about two major categories: oral literature (folktales, songs, proverbs told by word of mouth) and written literature (books, novels, poems)

subject: English Language
context: Learning about African and non-African folktales with features like didactic lessons (teaching morals), entertainment, magical elements, and animal characters, identifying themes, retelling folktales, and understanding their cultural significance

subject: English Language
context: Understanding myths (traditional stories explaining natural phenomena or beliefs, often involving gods and supernatural beings) and legends (stories about historical or semi-historical people and events), identifying their features like magic, supernatural elements, and superstition

subject: English Language
context: Learning about prose as written or spoken language in ordinary form (not poetry), understanding narrative prose (telling a story) and descriptive prose (describing something), and identifying features like plot, characterization, setting, theme, and style

subject: English Language
context: Understanding different types of poetry (epic, lyric, dramatic), learning about poetic language (concise, unique, elevated), recognizing rhythm and rhyme, explaining meanings of simple poems, and writing simple poems

subject: English Language
context: Learning about drama as stories performed on stage, understanding types (comedy - funny plays, tragedy - serious/sad plays), and identifying features like theme, stage, costumes, director, audience, and performance

subject: English Language
topic: Figures of Speech - Similes and Metaphors
context: Understanding similes as comparisons using 'like' or 'as' (She is as red as a rose) and metaphors as direct comparisons without 'like' or 'as' (She is a red rose), learning to identify and create both in sentences and literature

subject: English Language
context: Building vocabulary by learning to use context clues (surrounding words and sentences) to figure out meanings of unfamiliar words, understanding synonyms (words with similar meanings) and antonyms (words with opposite meanings)

subject: English Language
context: Learning to construct complete sentences with subject (who or what the sentence is about) and predicate (what the subject does or is), understanding basic sentence patterns, and using correct punctuation (capital letters, periods, question marks)

subject: English Language
context: Learning to write summaries by identifying main points in passages, removing unnecessary details, using own words to condense information, and writing brief coherent summaries that capture the essential meaning of longer texts
